summary: Stancil argues that despite the rough edges and hype, the modern data stack represents a huge leap forward from the brittle, expensive, and manual systems of the early 2010s. Yet he highlights a paradox: data teams delivered substantial business impact even with inferior tooling, so the key question is why today’s far better tech hasn’t produced proportional value gains.
money_quotes: The tools we have today—built and supported by thousands of people across dozens of companies—represent a profound leap forward from what we had then.
Yammer’s data team was as impactful as any that I’ve ever worked with.
Why has data technology advanced so much further than value a data team provides?
Does all of this new tooling actually hurt, by causing us to lose focus on the most important problems... in favor of the shiny new things that don’t actually matter?
But if our 2032 selves want to be as grateful for 2020s as we should be for the 2010s, those are the next questions we need to answer.
key_insight: Modern data tooling is dramatically better, but the industry still hasn’t closed the gap between technological progress and real business impact.
